Toss Update:  won the toss and elected to field first! 

The toss to win pattern refuses to fade in TATA IPL 2026. In the last five matches, every captain winning the toss has chosen to field and chased down targets with comfort, highlighted by DC pulling off a successful chase despite losing four wickets inside the powerplay. So what unfolds tonight? Will someone finally buck the trend, or does the streak roll on? Toss and team details coming up shortly.


PLAYERS TO WATCH OUT:


Ishan Kishan and Finn Allen set up an explosive player battle in the SRH vs KKR clash, based on their World Cup 2026 form. Kishan scored 317 runs in 9 matches at a strike rate of 193.3, with an exceptional 75.7% boundary percentage, showing his ability to combine aggression with consistency. Finn Allen was equally destructive, smashing 298 runs in 8 matches at a staggering strike rate of 200, with 73.8% of his runs in boundaries, highlighting his powerplay dominance. While Allen brings instant impact and chaos, Kishan offers sustained pressure. Whichever batter controls the early overs could define today’s match.
 

BATTLES TO WATCH OUT FOR:


Several intriguing individual battles could shape the outcome of the KKR vs SRH clash. Sunrisers Hyderabad’s Jaydev Unadkat has dismissed the skipper of Kolkata four times, making him a threat in powerplay. On the other hand, Ishan Kishan has faced Vaibhav Arora just once for 11 runs off 10 balls, but has been dismissed twice, highlighting a potential advantage for the young KKR pacer. These micro-contests, particularly between Klaasen and Narine in the spin-friendly middle overs and Kishan against Arora in the powerplay, could prove decisive in determining which side gains control in a high-scoring encounter.
 

HEAD-TO-HEAD:


Since 2023, Kolkata Knight Riders have had the upper hand against Sunrisers Hyderabad, winning five of their seven encounters, while SRH have managed just two victories. KKR’s dominance in recent seasons highlights their tactical familiarity with SRH, particularly at Eden Gardens, and underscores the psychological advantage they carry going into their matches.
 

VENUE STATS:


Eden Gardens in Kolkata has traditionally favored high-scoring matches, with teams batting first winning seven times and sides chasing prevailing in six encounters. The average first-innings score at the venue is 199, with teams posting around 58 runs in the powerplay and facing a stiff challenge in the death overs, where the run rate climbs to 12.2. The highest successful chase recorded here is 262/2, highlighting the batting-friendly nature of the pitch, especially under lights when dew plays a role. Pace bowlers have been more successful overall, accounting for 68% of wickets, while spinners have claimed the remaining 32%, mainly exploiting the middle overs. Teams batting first aim to set a big total, but chasing sides have an almost equal chance of victory, making strategic planning for toss and bowling rotations crucial at this historically iconic stadium.
 

Kolkata Knight Riders host Sunrisers Hyderabad at Eden Gardens on Thursday, 2 April, in what promises to be a high-scoring encounter. Both teams are looking to recover after their opening games, where they posted 200+ totals but failed to defend, exposing vulnerabilities in their bowling attacks.

KKR, led by Ajinkya Rahane, will play their first home game of the season. Rahane marks his 200th IPL match, a fitting milestone at a venue where KKR have historically dominated SRH. Finn Allen, in sensational form after a 33-ball hundred in the T20 World Cup semi-final at the same ground, is the key threat at the top. The KKR spin duo of Sunil Narine and Varun Chakravarthy is expected to exploit Eden Gardens’ middle overs, historically a spinner-friendly phase. The main concern for KKR remains the absence of frontline pacers Matheesha Pathirana and Cameron Green, who cannot bowl.

SRH, led by Ishan Kishan in Pat Cummins’ absence, also face bowling challenges. Harshal Patel carries the bulk of the pace responsibility, while Jaydev Unadkat and David Payne provide support. Their batting lineup, anchored by Klaasen and Travis Head, will need to fire to counter KKR’s spin threat. Eden Gardens’ dew factor may assist the chasing side, making the toss crucial.

With home advantage, a settled spin duo, and in-form top-order batsmen, KKR are slight favorites, while SRH must rely on big individual performances to turn the tide.
MatchSummary.PostMatchCommentary:
Nitish Kumar Reddy (Player of the Match):
"It feels really good because this is the match winning performance I had been waiting for over a long time and I am thankful it came at the right moment. I gained a lot of confidence not only with the bat but also with the ball which meant a lot to me. Coming through injuries was mentally tough with many negative thoughts since I could not bowl last season. I worked very hard on my bowling during the break before the IPL and it is satisfying to see that effort paying off on the field. My body is feeling great now and full credit goes to the trainers and physios who have taken excellent care of me."

???????????????????? ???????????????????????????? ???????? ???? pic.twitter.com/1XYvwWY30a

— SunRisers Hyderabad (@SunRisers) April 2, 2026


Ishan Kishan (Sunrisers Hyderabad Captain):
"I was not happy with my own performance but I was really pleased with how the rest of the team committed themselves in the middle and brought great energy which was exactly what I asked for before the game. I did not feel the pitch was very good to bat on but the way Head and Abhishek attacked in the powerplay made things easier and gave everyone time to understand the surface. Credit goes to all our batters especially Klaasen for finishing it off and Nitish for playing a lovely supporting role. Our plan in the second innings was always to hunt wickets because Finn can change games quickly and getting him early helped us. All the bowlers executed the plans really well and that made me very happy as a captain."

 

Ajinkya Rahane (Kolkata Knight Riders Captain):

"I felt we needed one or two big partnerships while chasing because the batters who were set had to take the game deeper. With the ball we did well in the final overs to pull things back, which was a positive for us. In the second innings, the pitch surprised us a bit as the ball held and gripped more than expected but that is no excuse. The middle overs were crucial, and that is where we needed stronger partnerships, while credit goes to the SRH bowlers for using their angles smartly. Moving forward, winning home games is vital, and we need to go back to the drawing board quickly with better awareness and possibly an extra batter. This was just an off day, and we will bounce back."


Sunrisers Hyderabad delivered one of their most dominant batting performances of the season at Eden Gardens, piling up a formidable 226 for 8 and leaving Kolkata Knight Riders with far too much to chase under sustained pressure. The innings was powered by brutal intent at the top, with Travis Head smashing 46 off just 21 balls and Abhishek Sharma matching him stroke for stroke with 48 from 21 as Hyderabad raced to 82 inside the first 5.4 overs.

The momentum did not slow once the early wickets fell. Heinrich Klaasen anchored the middle phase with a commanding 52 off 35 balls, striking at nearly 150, while Nitish Kumar Reddy supplied late acceleration with 39 from 24 to push the run rate above eleven. Hyderabad scored at over ten runs an over in 15 of the 20 overs, underlining the relentlessness of their approach, even as Blessing Muzarabani finished with four wickets amid the damage.

Kolkata’s reply began in whirlwind fashion with Finn Allen blasting 28 off seven balls, but the chase quickly lost shape as wickets fell in clusters. Angkrish Raghuvanshi stood firm with a fighting 52 from 29, yet with the asking rate above fourteen, pressure mounted relentlessly.

Jaydev Unadkat’s three for 21 and disciplined support from the rest ensured Kolkata were bowled out for 161 in just 16 overs. The result was decided by Hyderabad’s sustained scoring power, turning a competitive contest into a one sided spectacle marked by sheer batting supremacy.
